semantics: The StreamingCompiler lifecycle is created before the first Response body pull and remains owned by the calling VM until success, failure, or Context teardown.
Each normalized string or BufferSource chunk is copied into the compiler lifecycle exactly once and in pull order; no aggregate body handoff occurs after EOF.
String chunks use the Fetch Body UTF-8 conversion, BufferSource chunks reject detached backing, and zero-length chunks are accepted as exact no-ops.
Chunk normalization and every user-observable stream operation happen before the compiler lock is entered; the lock only protects native registry lookup and byte copying.
EOF finalizes the lifecycle exactly once, obtains one stable engine-owned ArrayBuffer, releases the opaque handle, and queues the existing ordinary compile or instantiate job.
Source rejection, abrupt pulls, invalid chunks, sticky compiler failure, setup failure, finalization failure, and asynchronous allocation failure abort and release the lifecycle exactly once.

An abandoned outer Promise does not cancel the standard active Fetch Body read; its managed reaction graph retains the operation until settlement, while Context teardown retires any remaining compiler record.

Response status, exact application/wasm MIME validation, bodyUsed, locking, sibling-realm acceptance, imports, feature selection, diagnostics, result shapes, and error classes remain those of the pinned Web API contract.
Focused tests observe three actual MVP body spans and two actual spans for SIMD, threads, exception-handling, memory64, and GC/Core 3 modules, including a precise-GC request between pulls.
The focused streaming compilation gate passes in Debug, ReleaseSafe, and TSan without running the complete WebAssembly corpora or changing accepted benchmark and conformance scores.
